title: Camera-enabled scalable homodyne detection of multimode quantum light
authors: Young-Do Yoon, Chan Roh, Geunhee Gwak, Young-Sik Ra
abstract: Scalability is a key challenge in advancing quantum technologies such as quantum computing, communication, and metrology. Photonic systems offer a promising route to scalability by enabling the deterministic generation of large-scale entangled states. Homodyne detection is an essential quantum measurement to exploit such entangled states, enabling quantum-enhanced measurement, deterministic quantum teleportation, GKP-state breeding, and quantum error correction. Despite the recent progress in generating large-scale quantum states, realizing quantum measurement at scale remains a major challenge. Here we realize scalable and efficient homodyne detection by leveraging a large number of pixels in a charge-coupled-device (CCD) camera. Our approach enables shot-noise-limited quadrature measurements of 60 optical modes simultaneously, while requiring only nanowatt-level local oscillator power per mode -- a six-order-of-magnitude reduction compared to conventional methods. The system achieves clearance exceeding 24 dB for all modes with negligible crosstalk. We demonstrate its compatibility with a large-scale quantum state by directly observing squeezing and entanglement in 60 optical modes. Furthermore, we showcase applications in verifying multipartite entanglement and in the conditional preparation of multimode states. This work provides a scalable method for quantum measurement, paving the way for large-scale quantum information processing.
summary: The paper presents a novel, camera-enabled homodyne detection technique to overcome the scalability limitations of measuring quantum light. By utilizing CCD arrays, they can simultaneously measure quadrature outcomes for dozens of optical modes using extremely low local oscillator power. This breakthrough enables direct experimental verification of entanglement and squeezing in large-scale multimode quantum states.
main_problem: The primary challenge addressed is achieving scalable, efficient quantum measurement, specifically homodyne detection, necessary for large-scale quantum technologies.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated a camera-based system capable of simultaneously measuring quadrature outcomes for 60 optical modes with significantly reduced local oscillator power and negligible crosstalk.
method: They leverage the pixel array of a CCD camera to perform weighted summations of photocurrents, enabling simultaneous measurement in a superposed mode basis without extra interferometers.
model_or_system: The system uses a camera-coupled homodyne detector setup to measure quantum light generated by sources like SPOPOs. It is designed to handle multimode states and test entanglement criteria on these measured quadratures.
key_observables: Quadrature outcomes ($\hat{x}, \hat{p}$), squeezing variances, entanglement indicators (e.g., nullifier variances), and correlations in superposed modes.
important_parameters: Scalability to 60+ modes; nanowatt-level local oscillator power per mode (a six-order-of-magnitude reduction); clearance exceeding 24 dB.
main_assumptions: The method assumes shot-noise-limited operation is achieved, and the low LO power is attributed to the CCD camera's readout characteristics.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the camera-based detection scheme mapping modes to pixels, show noise measurements for superposed modes testing entanglement criteria (Duan criterion), and detail protocols for verifying multipartite entangled states like GHZ and cluster states.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scalability problem in quantum measurement, details the novel camera-based homodyne setup, demonstrates its ability to measure nonclassical noise (squeezing/entanglement) in multimode light, and finally applies this technique to verify multipartite entanglement and perform conditional state preparation.
why_interesting: This work provides a highly scalable experimental platform for quantum measurement that directly addresses a major bottleneck in realizing large-scale quantum information processing protocols like quantum error correction or complex quantum simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Camera-based homodyne detection. a, Experimental scheme. A quantum state of light in multiple frequency modes, ˆρ, is interfered with a local oscillator (LO) at a 50:50 beam splitter (BS). The two output beams are diffracted by gratings and focused by lenses in a 2f configuration. As a result, a corresponding pair of camera pixels—one from each output beam and located on the left and right halves of the camera—maps each frequency-bin mode in b. During digital processing, the camera counts from each pixel pair—after gain balancing and weighted summation using the weight function in c—are subtracted and then normalized. Extending this procedure to all pixel pairs yields the quadrature...

FIG. 2. Homodyne detection of multimode quantum light. a, Experimental scheme. A multimode quantum state ˆρ, generated from a synchronously pumped optical parametric oscillator (SPOPO), is measured using a camera-based multimode homodyne detector (MHD). Quadrature outcomes for the 60 frequency-bin modes in Fig. 1b are simultaneously obtained as varying the LO phase θ. SHG: second harmonic generation. In b-d, we consider 30 pairs of the frequency-bin modes, (30 + k, 31 −k) for 1 ≤k ≤30. b, Quadrature outcomes for symmetric (blue, (30 + k, 31 −k)+) and antisymmetric (red, (30 + k, 31 −k)−) superpositions of two frequency-bin modes. These outcomes are obtained by adding (the symmetric case) or...

FIG. 3. Homodyne detection of multipartite entangled states. Left (a,d,g): a three-mode GHZ state, Middle (b,e,h): a four-mode GHZ state, Right (c,f,i): a (locally equivalent) four-mode square cluster state. a-c, Frequency-mode basis for each state. We use the frequency bins in Fig. 1b, grouping each pair of adjacent bins from the 15th to the 46th into a single mode, while discarding alternate pairs to improve spectral mode separation. d-f, Correlations of three different quadrature measurements (black dots). Two-dimensional projections are shown as red dots for the ˆx quadrature and blue dots for the ˆp quadrature. g-i, Quadrature correlations used to identify nullifier noise. The...

FIG. 4. Conditional preparation of multimode states. a, Multiple EPR pairs of modes are shared between Alice and Bob. Using a multimode homodyne detector (MHD), Alice obtains multimode quadrature outcomes (qA 1 , qA 2 , . . . , qA n ) at measurement angles of (θ1, θ2, . . . , θn). After Bob applies a displacement operator ˆDk(αk) at each mode k, a multimode squeezed state is prepared and subsequently measured with an MHD for verification. Note that the effect of the displacement operators can also be reproduced by post-processing the quadrature outcomes at Bob. b, Quadrature measurement of conditionally prepared three-mode states.
